title: Botox on Older Patients. How to Get a Great Result.
filename: Botox on Older Patients. How to Get a Great Result..en.vtt
excerpt: I've been doing a few videos on Botox brow list recently and a lot of people are asking about older patients are much trickier when it comes to lifting eyebrows for a number of reasons typically it's because as you age there are multiple variables of change and patients are extre…

segments.text: I've been doing a few videos on Botox brow list recently and a lot of people are asking about older patients are much trickier when it comes to lifting eyebrows for a number of reasons typically it's because as you age there are multiple variables of change and patients are extremely different so you'll have some patients for example who have hyperactive eyebrows lots of movement and if you do a brow lift on them they can actually become exaggerated so your spot brow type or Joker type eyebrows that are just hyper elevated is actually easier to do in an older patient than in a younger patient and that's because they lack the fat pads that cause a cushioning of the muscle so the muscle is unopposed and as you increase the resting tone of the muscle without any fat to resist it you can get a much more arched eyebrow the problem with these patients is then when you try and soften that it's actually quite easy sometimes to cause the other problem which is they drop and this is what makes eyebrow lifts with botchline Toxin and older people more difficult of course you can do it I've had many great results in

patients that older which I would classify as anyone 55 and older they become harder to treat but it's still possible and really the Nuance with this comes with getting to know the varieties of anatomy the types of patients and the difference that happens with different kind of volumetric changes in the face some people have excess fat pads as they get older and others get much thinner and they're very different to treat I would say it's probably easier to treat patients who are have excess fat pads because all that you get then is a slightly disappointing result whereas the patients with very little fat in their face you can cause quite shocking results that all their friends and family talk about in a bad way and this is really worth thinking about how do we get around these two different scenarios well the first is an older patient who you don't think you've got much chance of lifting you'll probably find in most cases just to be completely honest and say you may not get a great eyebrow lift but I can do the injection patterns that should cause an eyebrow lift at the worst case scenario is it'll

soften some of your lines and you'll get a very tiny lift but there isn't really much of a risk of causing an too much arching and someone who's is not that easy to lift the other patients are trickier because you've got this Balancing Act and they can ping pong between too much lift McDonald's shape eyebrows or the other way around where it collapses and they look heavy and sad and both make patients quite unhappy and the injector needs to learn about how to grade eyebrow lifts with practice knowing that you're leaving a certain amount of the frontalis untreated you can vary the amount of lift and you can almost fade it in it's a bit like hairdressers do with Great Haircuts they can fade them in and you can do the same thing with botram toxin particularly around the top of the Arches that we treat in the cat ear shape that I teach and then it's in these patients where you can learn with time how to vary the depth or the strength of the injection relative to the muscle that you want to leave untreated so you get varieties of softening of the eyebrow in its simplest form if you had someone

with a larger forehead who are easier to lift and you draw the cat ears and they go all the way to the top all you do is put one unit lower down in that section now if they still have a very strong muscle and they're very low in volume in their face they might still get too much activity and this is where you learn the little adjustments so you could put two one unit injections at lower down in the cat here and one at the top so you're fading it slightly more or you could put two units right in the middle of that cat ear and allow the spread of that toxin to change slightly as you get lower down towards the eye but it really comes down to finessing that area of muscle so that we're taking away just enough so you get a little bit of brow lift but it's not so strong that you get the ladder lines or as Millennials call it the Wi-Fi lines once you realize it's this finessing approach rather than an exact injection point you should over time figure this out by making small adjustments seeing the result and learning from it within each patient but also collectively between all your patients because you then get better at predicting what each patient needs so what do you do for an older patient who

has hooded eyelids it's very important to distinguish between actual eyelids versus a brow that's pressing on the same area because if you have skin that is connected all the way down to the eyelashes so there's one continuous sheet without this periorbital sulcus then you will end up with skin that rests on the eyelids that's about the frontalis muscle and those people you might be able to create some lift but they're also particularly easy to drop and make the problem worse so in particular if you ever see patients who want to treat horizontal lines and they have this shape of skin where the skin is continuous all the way to their eyelashes they're very easy to upset because if you cause a brow drop even a millimeter they will feel it so they are also the patients that if you cause a brow lift they're going to get some benefit you are there is a cohort of patients who have genuine eyelid drops so as you get older some patients eyelids actually get heavier and you you're unlikely to affect that at all by treating the frontalis or the corrugated

is all the orbicularis oculi there's a chance that you can cause a small lift by treating the actual orbicularisoculite that's on the eyelid as you would treat a ptosis some people are now doing this way if you get a ptosis you inject Botox near the eyelashes that's a potential way that you might cause a small lift but I've never seen it used so far in an older patient with an age-related eyelid ptosis but I can't see any reason why that wouldn't work um because it is affecting those muscles that balance the eyelids so do you need to make changes to dosages when injecting older patients in many areas of the face it's a very similar strength muscle with different levels of supporting tissue and this doesn't always make a difference I don't think it makes a great big difference when you're treating the LaBella on a 68 year old as opposed to 25 year olds similar dosages if you want a little bit of movement or no movement you're still going to have to treat in a similar way now that's very different with the frontalis and potentially with the orbicularis oculi because there's less support for the tissues in other

ways probably the best example is orbicularisocular because when you're treating crow's feet in an older patient and you just relax the muscle it's very easy to relax the muscle and improve the lines the problem is that as you get older the degree of support relative to the other structures that the muscle gives the cheek is higher essentially the orbicular circular is supporting the cheek and so if you use the same dose in a younger person it makes them look older they actually sag so you get the sad look as the cheek descends because the orbicularis oculi is no longer supporting the cheek in its resting position so that's the most important thing to think about with older people there's a point at which the muscle is so much more important for supporting the other structures so I include eyebrow and cheek that you would not want to treat it at all and you you'll get this feeling when you see a very old person whose skin is very lacks that botulin toxin on its own is probably although it will get rid of the line or the wrinkle it's going to cause a ptosis somewhere either a cheek or an or a brow ptosis even with very low

Doses and trust your instincts on that if there's no support there from others from other tissue it's better not to treat and of course then there's this gray area in between which is where experience really comes in you have to pay attention to patients the degree of skin laxity and how much Botox you put in would change according to the point where you should put very low doses in older people or even further beyond that they are not appropriate to treat at all particularly with frontalis and orbicularisocular so in summary I hope that's given you more to think about in terms of how you treat your older patients and acknowledge some of the difficulty with this area because it isn't as straightforward as treating our typical patients who are probably around 37 to 43 those older patients do require more thoughts and I hope I've given you some structure to approach that with thanks for watching don't forget there's a download that comes with this if you want to learn more about different Botox injecting patterns at the bottom of this video foreign
